theory.intro: A fraction only makes sense when you know the whole it refers to. Two fractions can only be compared or added when they describe the **same whole**.
facts: $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of a small pizza is **less** than $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of a large pizza — the wholes differ.
To compare or add fractions, the **whole must be the same size**.
The denominator names how many equal parts the **one whole** is split into.
steps: Identify the whole each fraction refers to.
Check the wholes are the same size.
Only then compare, add or subtract the fractions.

foundation.q: Is $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of a whole always the same amount as $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of a different-sized whole?
foundation.a: No — the wholes must be the same size

foundation.q: Two chocolate bars are different sizes. Amy eats $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of the small bar, Ben eats $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of the large bar. Did they eat the same amount of chocolate?
Can you add $\tfrac{1}{4}$ of one cake and $\tfrac{1}{4}$ of a much bigger cake to get $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of one cake?
$\tfrac{1}{3}$ of a $300$ mL glass and $\tfrac{1}{3}$ of a $600$ mL glass. Which is more juice?
What does the denominator $5$ in $\tfrac{3}{5}$ tell you?
foundation.a: The whole is split into $5$ equal parts

foundation.q: Two identical sheets of paper. One is shaded $\tfrac{2}{4}$, the other $\tfrac{1}{2}$. Same whole — is the shaded amount the same?
Is it fair to say '$\tfrac{1}{2}$ is bigger than $\tfrac{3}{4}$' if the halves come from a giant cake and the quarters from a tiny cake?
foundation.a: No — fractions must refer to the same whole

foundation.q: A pizza is cut into $8$ equal slices. What is the whole pizza as a fraction of itself?
Two glasses are the same size. One is $\tfrac{1}{4}$ full, the other $\tfrac{2}{4}$ full. Can you compare them directly?
Why can $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of a class and $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of a different-sized class be different numbers of students?

development.q: $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of a $40$ cm ribbon vs $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of a $60$ cm ribbon. Find both lengths.
Sam ate $\tfrac{3}{4}$ of a small chocolate bar; Lily ate $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of a large bar twice the size. Who ate more, in 'small bars'?
A whole is divided into $6$ equal parts. How many parts make $\tfrac{2}{3}$ of the whole?
Why is $\tfrac{1}{3} + \tfrac{1}{3}$ of the same cake meaningful, but $\tfrac{1}{3}$ of cake A $+\ \tfrac{1}{3}$ of cake B is not a single fraction of one cake?
$\tfrac{1}{4}$ of a $\$80$ phone bill vs $\tfrac{1}{4}$ of a $\$120$ bill. How much is each share?
Two pizzas are the same size, cut into $8$ and $4 $ slices. Are $4$ small slices the same as $2$ large slices?
$\tfrac{2}{5}$ of a tank holds $20$ L. The wholes are the same tank. How much is $\tfrac{1}{5}$?
A recipe uses $\tfrac{1}{2}$ cup of sugar. A friend uses $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of a larger measuring cup. Will they add the same amount?
If $\tfrac{3}{8}$ of a whole is shaded, what fraction of the same whole is unshaded?
Two rulers, $20$ cm and $30$ cm. Each marked at $\tfrac{1}{2}$. Which $\tfrac{1}{2}$ mark is further from $0$?

mastery.q: Jade says $\tfrac{1}{4}$ of her cake is more than $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of mine. Could she be right? Explain.
mastery.a: Yes, if her cake is more than twice the size of mine

solution.math: $\text{possible if her cake is large enough}$

mastery.q: A class of $24$ and a class of $36$ each have $\tfrac{1}{3}$ absent. How many absent in total?
Two tanks: one holds $50$ L, one holds $100$ L. The small tank is $\tfrac{4}{5}$ full, the large $\tfrac{1}{2}$ full. Which holds more water?
mastery.a: The large tank ($50$ L vs $40$ L)

mastery.q: Explain why '$\tfrac{1}{2} > \tfrac{1}{3}$' is only guaranteed true when both fractions refer to the same whole.
mastery.a: Different wholes can reverse the comparison

mastery.q: A baker has a $1$ kg loaf and a $2$ kg loaf. She sells $\tfrac{3}{4}$ of the small and $\tfrac{1}{4}$ of the large. Which sale is more bread?
mastery.a: The small loaf ($750$ g vs $500$ g)

mastery.q: $\tfrac{2}{3}$ of bottle A equals $\tfrac{1}{3}$ of bottle B. What can you say about the bottle sizes?
mastery.a: Bottle B is twice the size of bottle A

mastery.q: Two pizzas, $8$ and $12$ slices, same size. Are $3$ slices of the first equal to $4$ slices of the second?
A budget of $\$600$ and one of $\$900$ each spend $\tfrac{2}{3}$. Find the total spent.
If $\tfrac{1}{2}$ of jar X holds the same as the whole of jar Y, how do the jars compare?
mastery.a: Jar X is twice as big as jar Y

mastery.q: A whole is the same in both: $\tfrac{3}{5} + \tfrac{1}{5}$ shaded, the rest white. What fraction is white?
